era_context: The late 15th century is characterized by European maritime expansion, Ottoman consolidation in the eastern Mediterranean and Balkans, Ming stability and maritime dominance in Asia, and the final phases of the Iberian Reconquista. This year falls during the early Age of Exploration, with Portuguese and Spanish expeditions actively seeking new trade routes. The Italian Wars are ongoing, and European political fragmentation continues despite the rise of centralized monarchies.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan African interior (limited written sources; Portuguese coastal records exist but are sparse)
Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond Portuguese contact
Pacific Islands and Polynesia (no written records)
North American interior (no European contact; indigenous records non-existent in written form)

events.title: Vasco da Gama departs Portugal on voyage to India
events.description: Vasco da Gama, Portuguese navigator, departs Lisbon on 8 July 1497 with a fleet of four ships to find a sea route to India. The expedition is sponsored by King Manuel I of Portugal and represents the culmination of decades of Portuguese maritime exploration along the African coast. Da Gama's voyage will take him around the Cape of Good Hope and across the Indian Ocean, arriving in Calicut in May 1499. This voyage marks the beginning of the direct European maritime route to Asia, bypassing Ottoman-controlled Middle Eastern trade routes and fundamentally reshaping global trade networks. The voyage is one of the most significant maritime achievements of the age.
key_figures: Bartolomeu Dias (predecessor who rounded the Cape)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and expedition documentation
Roteiro da Viagem de Vasco da Gama (Roteiro), contemporary account by expedition member
Historiographic consensus from Portuguese maritime history

events.certainty_note: The departure date of 8 July 1497 is well-attested in Portuguese royal records and the Roteiro. This is one of the most thoroughly documented voyages of the era.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: John Cabot explores the coast of North America
events.region: North America, Atlantic coast (modern Newfoundland, Nova Scotia)
events.description: John Cabot (Giovanni Caboto), Italian explorer in English service, undertakes a voyage from Bristol, England, in May 1497 to explore the North American coast. Cabot's expedition reaches the coast of North America (likely Newfoundland or Nova Scotia) and explores the coastline, returning to England in August 1497. The voyage establishes English claims to North America and demonstrates that a northern route across the Atlantic is feasible. Cabot's voyage is less well-documented than Columbus's expeditions, but it represents an important early European exploration of the North American continent. The voyage lays the foundation for later English colonial claims in North America.
key_figures: John Cabot (Giovanni Caboto, Italian explorer in English service)

sources.name: English royal records and letters patent
Contemporary accounts by English merchants and officials
Later compilations of exploration accounts (Hakluyt, Purchas)

events.certainty_note: The voyage date (May–August 1497) is accepted by most scholars, though some details of Cabot's exact route and landfall remain debated. The voyage is less thoroughly documented than Columbus's expeditions, and some sources place it in 1498.

events.title: Cornish Rebellion in England
events.description: A rebellion erupts in Cornwall, England, in response to heavy taxation imposed by King Henry VII to finance military campaigns in Scotland. The rebels, led by Michael An Gof and Thomas Flamank, march toward London to protest the tax burden. The rebellion is suppressed by royal forces at the Battle of Deptford Bridge (17 June 1497), with the rebel leaders captured and executed. The rebellion reflects discontent among common people over taxation and royal policies, but it is quickly crushed by superior royal military force. The event demonstrates the growing power of centralized English monarchy and the limits of popular resistance to royal authority.
events.certainty_note: The rebellion date (1497) and the Battle of Deptford Bridge (17 June 1497) are well-documented in English royal records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Treaty of Ayton between England and Scotland
events.description: The Treaty of Ayton (30 September 1497) establishes a peace agreement between England and Scotland, ending hostilities and establishing a marriage alliance between the English and Scottish royal families. The treaty provides for the marriage of King James IV of Scotland to Margaret Tudor, daughter of King Henry VII of England. The treaty marks a significant diplomatic achievement for both kingdoms and establishes a framework for peaceful relations between England and Scotland. The marriage alliance will eventually lead to the union of the English and Scottish crowns in 1603, though this is not foreseen at the time of the treaty.
events.certainty_note: The treaty date (30 September 1497) and terms are well-documented in both English and Scottish diplomatic records.
cross_references: 1503-002 (Marriage of James IV and Margaret Tudor)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet undertakes fifth voyage from China
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: The fifth voyage of Zheng He's treasure fleet departs from China, continuing the pattern of massive maritime expeditions that have characterized the early Ming period. The fleet explores the Indian Ocean, visiting East African ports, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Persian Gulf. The voyage demonstrates continued Chinese maritime capability and diplomatic reach. The expeditions are primarily diplomatic and trade missions, aimed at demonstrating Ming power and collecting tribute from foreign rulers. The voyages do not result in permanent territorial expansion or the establishment of trading monopolies.

sources.name: Ming court records and imperial archives (Mingshi)
The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingyai Shenglan) by Ma Huan
Historiographic consensus from Chinese maritime history

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Zheng He's voyages remains debated among scholars. The 1497 date for the fifth voyage is approximate, as sources do not provide precise departure and return dates for individual voyages.

events.title: Perkin Warbeck's rebellion in England ends
events.description: Perkin Warbeck, a pretender to the English throne who claimed to be Richard, Duke of York (one of the 'Princes in the Tower'), is captured and executed in November 1497. Warbeck had been supported by various European powers, including Scotland and the Holy Roman Empire, in his attempt to challenge King Henry VII's rule. His capture and execution mark the end of a significant threat to Henry VII's authority and consolidate Tudor control over England. The event demonstrates Henry VII's success in establishing stable rule after the Wars of the Roses and his ability to neutralize rival claimants to the throne.
key_figures: King James IV of Scotland (former supporter)
Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor (former supporter)

sources.name: Scottish and Holy Roman Empire records

events.certainty_note: The execution date of Perkin Warbeck (November 1497) is well-documented in English royal records and contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: 1485-001 (Henry VII becomes king after Battle of Bosworth)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Zheng He's voyages remains contested among scholars, with some historians arguing that the traditional 'seven voyages' framework is a later historiographic construction. The significance of John Cabot's voyage is complicated by the lack of detailed contemporary documentation—some scholars argue that the voyage may have occurred in 1498 rather than 1497, and the exact location of Cabot's landfall remains debated. Additionally, the narrative of Cabot as a triumphant discoverer is undermined by the fact that his voyage had limited immediate impact on English colonial expansion, which did not materialize until the 16th century.
historiographic_note: The year 1497 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding maritime exploration and English politics. However, this creates a significant Eurocentric bias: European activities are extensively chronicled while contemporary developments in Asia, Africa, and the Americas outside European contact zones are underrepresented. Ottoman and Ming sources exist but are less accessible to Western historians. Indigenous American perspectives are entirely absent from written records. The historical record is shaped by the interests of literate elites and merchants, with little representation of common people or non-literate societies. Portuguese, English, and Spanish sources dominate the exploration narrative, while the perspectives of Indian, East African, and other non-European peoples are largely absent or filtered through European accounts.

graph_edges.note: Da Gama's departure in 1497 led to his arrival in Calicut in 1499, establishing the sea route to India
Cabot's 1497 voyage established English claims to North America, which eventually led to English colonial expansion in the 17th century
The Cornish Rebellion and the Treaty of Ayton both occurred in 1497, reflecting Henry VII's consolidation of power
The Treaty of Ayton in 1497 provided for the marriage of James IV and Margaret Tudor, which took place in 1503
Warbeck's execution in 1497 is part of Henry VII's consolidation of power following his victory at Bosworth in 1485
